content: Peripheral neuropathy refers to damage of peripheral nerves, affecting ~2.4% of general population, up to 8% of elderly. Classification: by distribution (mononeuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, polyneuropathy), by fiber type (sensory, motor, autonomic; large-fiber vs small-fiber), by pathology (axonal degeneration vs demyelination). Common causes: diabetes (most common, distal symmetric sensorimotor), alcohol, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, renal failure, medications (chemotherapy, amiodarone, metronidazole), autoimmune (CIDP, vasculitis), hereditary (Charcot-Marie-Tooth), infections (HIV, Lyme, leprosy), toxins. Small-fiber neuropathy affects pain/temperature fibers (burning pain, autonomic symptoms) with normal NCS; diagnosed by skin biopsy showing reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density. Large-fiber neuropathy affects proprioception/vibration, causes sensory ataxia, areflexia, abnormal NCS.
